title: Beef stroganoff
subtitle: Beef strips and mushrooms in a soured cream sauce
excerpt: Thin strips of sirloin or rump steak seared quickly over high heat and finished in a sauce of sautéed onion, mushrooms, a little mustard, and soured cream. Served over egg noodles or rice. A fast, rich dish that relies on a very hot pan and brief cooking to keep the beef tender.
sourceNotes: Beef stroganoff takes its name from a nineteenth-century Russian noble family; the dish appears in Russian cookbooks from the mid-nineteenth century. It entered Western cookery through French culinary influence in Russia and became widespread in the West after the Second World War, with the soured cream version becoming the international standard. It is documented in mid-twentieth-century American home-cooking collections.

content.text: The beef must be seared, not braised. The difference is heat and timing: a very hot pan, beef cut into strips no thicker than 1 cm, 60 seconds per batch at most. If the pan is not hot enough, or if you add too much beef at once, the meat will steam and turn grey and tough before the sauce has even started. Cook in two or three batches if necessary and keep the cooked strips warm while you make the sauce in the same pan.

The soured cream is stirred in at the very end, off the heat or over the lowest possible setting. Boiling soured cream causes it to split. The sauce should be just warm enough to serve, not simmering. Taste before you finish — the mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and the fond left by the beef all contribute, and you may need very little additional salt.

items.prepNote: sliced into thin strips against the grain
egg noodles, or serve with rice

content.text: Season the {{beef-sirloin}} of beef strips with salt and pepper. Heat a thin layer of oil in the frying pan over very high heat until it just begins to smoke. Add the beef in a single layer — work in batches to avoid crowding. Sear for 30 to 60 seconds without stirring, then toss once and cook for a further 30 seconds. Remove to a plate. Repeat with remaining beef.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, then the {{onion}} and {{garlic}}. Cook for 4 to 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} and cook for a further 4 minutes until golden. Pour in the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, scraping up any browned bits, and simmer for 2 minutes. Add the {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce.

Turn the heat to its lowest setting or remove the pan from the heat entirely. Return the beef and any resting juices to the pan. Stir in the {{soured-cream}} of soured cream until the sauce is smooth and pale. Do not allow it to boil. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately over egg noodles or rice.

items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or too much meat added at once — the temperature dropped and the meat steamed
items.fix: Use the highest heat your hob produces and cook in small batches; 60 seconds total per batch is all the beef needs

items.symptom: Soured cream splits and looks grainy
items.cause: Sauce was simmering when soured cream was added
items.fix: Remove from the heat or turn to the lowest setting before adding soured cream; stir it in gently and do not reheat once added

items.cause: Stock not reduced enough, or mushrooms gave out too much water
items.fix: Cook the mushrooms until all their liquid has evaporated before adding the stock; simmer the stock for 2 to 3 minutes before adding the soured cream
The mustard and Worcestershire sauce provide depth — increase both slightly and check the salt level; a small squeeze of lemon juice added at the end can also lift the whole dish

content.text: Beef stroganoff has its origin in nineteenth-century Russian haute cuisine and takes its name from the Stroganov family, who were prominent Russian nobles and patrons. The dish appeared in Russian cookery books from the 1870s onward and spread through Western Europe via the exchange of French and Russian culinary traditions: French cuisine had enormous influence on Russian aristocratic cooking throughout the nineteenth century, and the dish reads as a hybrid of both. It entered American home cooking in the mid-twentieth century through post-war cookbooks and became a weeknight dinner staple, recognisably European in origin but adapted entirely into the American domestic kitchen, typically served over egg noodles rather than its original accompaniment of fried potato straws.
